text.content:
Ashen is a Dark Souls III one-shot running on the Dungeons & Dragons 5e engine. The rules you already know still apply — attacks, AC, saving throws, spells, death saves. We only change a handful of things to make it feel like Dark Souls. This page is the whole hack in one screen.

The five things that are different

  • Bonfires are your checkpoints. They are the only place you rest, and resting respawns the enemies. (See Bonfires.)
  • Estus is your healing. A flask with charges that refill at a bonfire — not Hit Dice, not healing spells. (See Estus.)
  • Souls are one shared pool. They are money AND experience at the same time, and you can lose the ones you are carrying. (See Souls & Death.)
  • Defense is a timing puzzle. When something hits you, you choose to Dodge, Block, or Parry, and when to do it. Reading the attack matters more than your stats. (See The Defensive Reaction System.)
  • Leveling is a single choice. Each level you pick +1 to an ability score OR one spell. No sheet-building. (See Attributes & Leveling.)

The golden rule: die, learn, overcome

You are supposed to lose fights. Death is cheap here — you wake up at the last bonfire and try again, a little wiser. Bosses do not get easier, but you get smarter: every wipe, the DM tells you a little more about what you are fighting. Beating a wall means either getting good (learning the tells) or getting strong (farming souls to level up). Both are valid. Both are Dark Souls.

Players: read The Defensive Reaction System before session one — it is the heart of combat. Everything else you can learn at the table.

A bonfire is the only safe place in the world. It is your checkpoint, your campfire, and your respawn point all at once.

Resting at a bonfire

Sitting at a bonfire is the only way to take a rest. One bonfire rest counts as a 5e long rest for the whole party. Resting:

  • Restores all Hit Points.
  • Refills every Estus Flask to full.
  • Refills every spell's casts to full.
  • Clears Exhaustion and status build-up (Frostbite, etc.).
  • Lets you spend banked souls to level up (only at the Firelink bonfire, where the Fire Keeper sits).

The catch: enemies come back

The moment you rest, every non-boss enemy in the area respawns. Hollows you already killed are standing again. This is the core tension of Dark Souls: rest to recover, but pay for it by re-clearing the path. Bosses do NOT respawn once dead.

Bonfires sit right outside boss doors

There is always a bonfire just before a fog gate (a boss arena entrance). If the party wipes to a boss, you restart at that bonfire — no long walk back. You lose the fight, not your evening.

Kindling (Embers)

If you have an Ember, you may use it at a bonfire to kindle — gaining bonus maximum HP until you die. See Status Effects for how Embers work.

Healing in Ashen runs on the Estus Flask. Cure spells exist for the holy classes, but the flask is everyone's lifeline.

The Estus Flask

  • You start with an Estus Flask holding 3 charges.
  • Drinking is an action: spend one charge to heal 2d4 + 2 HP.
  • The flask refills to full at any bonfire rest. Charges do not come back any other way.

Because charges only refill at bonfires, Estus is a resource you ration across a whole area, not a per-fight top-up. Burning all three flasks before the boss means facing the boss dry.

Upgrading the flask

Estus ShardFound or bought from the Shrine Handmaid. Permanently adds +1 charge to the flask (cap around 6).
Estus +NAndre the blacksmith can reinforce the flask, raising the heal to 2d4+4, then 2d4+6, and so on.

Shared sips

The flask is personal — each character has their own. You cannot drink from a downed ally's flask, so keep your own topped up at every bonfire.

Souls are the currency of the dead. In Ashen they are both your money and your experience points, and the party shares one single pool.

One shared pool

Every soul the party earns goes into a common pot tracked on the Party sheet. Anyone can spend from it. You level up from it, buy from it, and upgrade from it. Souls come in two states:

  • Banked souls — once you spend souls on a level or an item at a bonfire, that value is locked in and can never be lost.
  • Carried souls — souls you have earned but not yet spent are at risk.

Going down vs. wiping

There are two very different kinds of "death" here:

A single hero drops to 0 HPNormal 5e death saving throws. Allies can stabilize or heal you back up. Nothing is lost. This is a setback, not a respawn.
The whole party wipesEveryone respawns at the last bonfire. The boss/enemies reset. All carried souls drop as a bloodstain at the spot you died.

The bloodstain

On a wipe, your carried souls are left behind as a glowing bloodstain. Fight back to it and touch it to reclaim every soul. But if you wipe again before reaching it, the old bloodstain is gone forever and a new one forms where you just fell. Greed kills.

Lesson built into the math: spend your souls when you are safe at a bonfire. Hoarding a huge carried balance into a hard fight is how you lose it all.

Why death is cheap on purpose

Respawn is fast and your character is never permanently gone. That is what lets the world be genuinely deadly without being cruel — we can throw lethal, telegraphed danger at you precisely because losing only costs you progress and souls, never the character.

Cinders are the party’s lives — the last sparks of the First Flame you carry. They are what let an Unkindled rise from death instead of going Hollow for good. The whole party shares one pool.

How Cinders work

  • One shared pool. Every hero draws from the same Cinders. When anyone falls, the fire that lifts them comes from the common pool.
  • Spend one to rise. When a hero drops to 0 HP, the party can spend 1 Cinder to pull them back to their feet — a Souls “YOU DIED”, then up and fighting. On a full wipe, spend 1 Cinder to respawn together at the last bonfire.
  • They do not refill at a bonfire. HP, Estus and spell casts all come back when you rest — Cinders never do. They only ever go down.
  • When the Cinders run out, the fire can no longer lift you. The next fall is real: you must be saved the hard way, and a wipe with none left means the Flame goes out. Don’t let the pool hit zero before the end.

A Cinder is not an Ember. An Ember is a kindling item you burn at a bonfire for bonus HP; a Cinder is a life — your ability to rise at all.

Why it’s built this way

Death in Ashen is cheap on purpose — you respawn, you keep your character, you lose only ground and souls. Cinders are the one thing that makes death finally bite: a finite, shared, slowly-draining measure of how much fire the party has left. Spend them on bold plays and hard lessons; just don’t arrive at the final fight on empty.

This is the signature system of Ashen and the most important page for players. In Dark Souls you survive by reading attacks and reacting at the right moment — not by having big numbers. We model that directly.

Every attack has three windows

When an enemy attacks you, the DM narrates the move in three beats:

  • W1 — Wind-up: the telegraph. The boss rears the mace back over its shoulder.
  • W2 — Commit: the swing is mid-motion and committed.
  • W3 — Impact: the blow is landing.

You choose a defense AND a window

As the target, you declare one defense and which window you act in — for example "Dodge on W2" or "Parry on W3" or just "Block." Then you roll once and resolve.

Get gud, not stats

Here is the twist that makes it Dark Souls: your defensive d20 is rolled flat — you add NO ability modifier. The only thing that changes the roll is whether you read the move correctly:

  • Right defense, right window → roll with advantage.
  • A workable but not ideal choice → a normal roll.
  • Wrong read → roll with disadvantage.
  • Some choices are simply impossible against a given move (you cannot parry a boulder).

A clumsy Deprived who reads the tell will out-survive a nimble Mercenary who panics. Stats give you tools, not results — more reactions, sturdier blocks, passive AC for when you don't react — but they never roll the dodge for you.

Armor & passive defense

Your armor always reduces incoming damage: heavy plate armor (Knight's Plate, Chainmail) reduces damage by 2-3 points per hit; light armor by 1 point. This reduction is passive — it applies to every hit you take, whether you react or not, and stacks with dodges, blocks, and parries. The catch: heavier armor requires Strength to wear and imposes stealth disadvantage. Wearing nothing? No reduction, but you're faster and lighter.

The hidden grid (the puzzle)

Each enemy move has a secret Defensive Profile: a 3x3 grid of how good each defense is in each window. Learning that grid is the boss fight. A move's grid might look like this once you have figured it out:

W1 Wind-upW2 CommitW3 Impact
DodgenormalADVdisadv.
Block🛡️🛡️🛡️
Parrydisadv.disadv.ADV

You do not get this grid handed to you. You learn it by trying things, watching what works, and — when you wipe — hearing the next rung of the boss's Tell Ladder from the DM.

The three defenses

Dodge — always free, always available

Roll a flat d20 versus the move's Dodge DC, with quality set by your window read. Success = take no damage and reposition 5 feet. If that repositioning puts you behind the enemy, your next attack has advantage (footwork into a backstab). Failure = full damage. Dodge costs nothing and you can do it against every attack — it is your bread and butter. A few moves (grabs, tracking charges) are tagged undodgeable.

Block — costs your reaction, no roll

Raise your shield. No roll, fully deterministic. Your shield reduces each damage type by a set percentage printed on its card — a Knight Shield soaks most physical, a buckler far less. At low tiers a block chips rather than zeroing a hit, and a block never staggers the enemy (only a parry does that). Some moves — charges, grabs, shield-kicks, magic, true combos — are unblockable: don't wall them, sidestep the lane instead.

Grid notation: the Block row shows 🛡️ (your reduction applies) or — (unblockable). Dodge and Parry rows use ADV / normal / disadv. / — (impossible).

Parry — costs your reaction, high risk, high reward

Roll a flat d20 versus the move's Parry DC. Only one ideal window gives advantage; every other window is disadvantage. On a success you negate the hit AND stagger the enemy — it loses its next action and the whole party gets a free riposte against it. On a failure you take full damage with no reduction. Most moves are unparryable — only clean weapon swings and thrusts can be parried, exactly like the game.

Your reaction economy (this is why dodging is king)

  • Dodge is free and unlimited — use it every round, against every attack; it never touches your reaction.
  • Block and Parry draw from your single shared reaction (one per round).
  • Heavy armor & shields grant Poise; a fully-armored defender may gain a second reaction (the DM's call), so it can block and parry more often.

Mooks vs. bosses

The same system runs everywhere, only the size changes. A regular enemy has exactly ONE move — you solve its tiny grid in a hit or two, so trivial fights stay fast and double as practice. A mini-boss has two or three moves; a major boss has four to six, drawn from its real Dark Souls moveset. The bigger the monster, the bigger the puzzle.

You begin at level 3 and climb toward level 10 over the one-shot. Leveling is deliberately fast — each level is one decision, made from the player-facing Level-Up chat card in under a minute.

What you get automatically every level

This part is not a choice — it just happens, so martial characters never feel empty:

  • Hit Points — every hero gains HP each level (your class hit-die average), applied automatically when the DM runs the Level Up macro.
  • Proficiency-bonus scaling at the normal 5e levels.
  • Extra Attack at level 5 for martial characters.

Your one choice each level: +1 to an ability score, OR a spell

On top of that backbone, each level you pick one of:

  1. +1 to one of your six ability scores — this raises the real score on your sheet, or
  2. one new spell of an unlocked tier (see Spellcasting).

That is the central trade-off. Every +1 makes you better immediately (a bigger modifier), and reaching 15 in a score unlocks that ability's milestone — a permanent power spike. So each level you choose to go tall (deepen your main stat) or wide (push a second stat toward its 15).

The six abilities & their milestones

Your modifier scales things the way 5e already does. On top of that, hitting 15 grants a milestone card automatically:

STR — Strengthmelee attack & damage15: Crushing Blow — +1 weapon damage die on melee hits (DM-adjudicated)
DEX — Dexterityfinesse/ranged attack & damage, AC15: Extra Strike — +1 attack on your Attack action, cap 3 (DM-adjudicated)
VIG — Vigoryour HP & toughness (this is CON)no milestone — and each Vigor pick also grants +5 HP, so it is the pure-survivability stat
INT — Intelligencesorceries: spell damage & save DC15: +1 cast to every sorcery you know
WIS — Wisdommiracles: heal/damage & save DC15: +1 cast to every miracle you know
IW — Inner Willpyromancies: fire damage & save DC (this is CHA)15: +1 cast to every pyromancy you know

Milestones are permanent and appear as a card on your sheet the moment you hit 15. Each pregen starts with its signature milestone already unlocked — the Knight has Crushing Blow, the Mercenary has Extra Strike, and the Sorcerer/Cleric/Pyromancer each have their caster milestone.

Spell tiers unlock by LEVEL

Spells come in three tiers; they become available purely by character level — the same for everyone:

  • Tier 1 from level 4.
  • Tier 2 from level 6.
  • Tier 3 from level 9.

A high casting score doesn't unlock tiers earlier — it makes your spells hit harder and, at 15, grants the milestone. (One real gate: a Tier-3 spell has 0 casts until you own your school's 15 milestone — see Spellcasting.)

Level-by-level cadence

L3 to 4+1 ability OR a Tier-1 spell
L4 to 5+1 ability OR a Tier-1 spell
L5 to 6T2 unlocks. +1 ability OR a spell (T1–T2)  ·  plus a Weapon Art
L6 to 7+1 ability OR a spell (T1–T2)
L7 to 8+1 ability OR a spell (T1–T2)
L8 to 9T3 unlocks. +1 ability OR a spell (T1–T3)  ·  plus a Weapon Art
L9 to 10+1 ability OR a spell (T1–T3)

Weapon Arts & Skills (L6 and L9)

At levels 6 and 9, everyone also picks a special Weapon Art from a shared menu — think Elden Ring ashes of war. The menu holds martial arts (Stomp, Perseverance, Spin Slash, Quickstep, Leo Riposte…) and caster arts (Steady Chant, Crystallize, Pyromancer's Fervor, Sage's Focus), plus the universal Estus Mastery. See the Level-Up Cards.

Costs (souls)

Leveling spends from the shared soul pool at the Fire Keeper: 500, 800, 1,200, 1,800, 2,600, 3,600, 5,000 souls for levels 4 through 10 respectively.

Magic in Ashen uses Dark Souls' three schools and a simple per-spell cast system instead of 5e spell slots.

Per-spell casts (how many times you can cast)

  • Every spell carries its own casts, shown right on its card, and refills them at a bonfire (never mid-fight).
  • Base casts by tier: Tier 1 = 2, Tier 2 = 1, Tier 3 = 0.
  • Your school's 15 milestone adds +1 to every spell of that school, so a milestone caster gets Tier 1 = 3, Tier 2 = 2, Tier 3 = 1.
  • Tier 3 is milestone-only: without your school's 15 milestone a Tier-3 spell sits at 0 casts and cannot be cast (you can still learn it and grow into it).

The three schools (and what you need to cast them)

Sorceriespowered by Intelligence (INT)require a Staff / Catalyst in hand
Miraclespowered by Wisdom (WIS)require a Talisman / Chime in hand
Pyromanciespowered by Inner Will (IW)require the Pyromancy Flame in hand

Catalysts are items — loot or bought from the Shrine Handmaid. Anyone holding the right catalyst can cast, regardless of class — that is very Dark Souls: a Knight who pushes Wisdom and carries a Talisman becomes a paladin. Your casting stat decides how hard the spell hits; raising it to 15 is what grants the extra cast (and any Tier-3 casting at all).

Tiers unlock by level

Tier access is by character level, the same for everyone: Tier 1 from level 4, Tier 2 from level 6, Tier 3 from level 9. A higher casting score never unlocks tiers earlier — it just makes your magic hit harder.

Tiers map to SRD spell levels

Every Dark Souls spell is a reflavor of a familiar SRD spell of matching power:

  • Tier 1 ≈ SRD level 1-2 (e.g., Soul Arrow = Magic Missile, Heal Aid = Cure Wounds, Fire Orb = Burning Hands).
  • Tier 2 ≈ SRD level 3-4 (e.g., Crystal Soul Spear = Lightning Bolt, Wrath of the Gods = Spirit Guardians, Fireball = Fireball).
  • Tier 3 ≈ SRD level 5 (e.g., White Dragon Breath = Cone of Cold, Great Lightning Spear = Flame Strike, Great Chaos Fire Orb = Flame Strike).

Spell attack & save DC

Your spell attack = proficiency + your school's ability modifier (INT for sorceries, WIS for miracles, IW for pyromancies). Your save DC = 8 + proficiency + that modifier. Raising the score lifts both your damage modifier and your DC, so a caster who deepens their stat scales smoothly.

The caster's mercy valve (you always chip)

A caster should never feel like dead weight on a bad roll. Damaging magic always does something:

  • Missed a spell attack? It still deals your spellcasting modifier in the spell's damage type (minimum 1).
  • Enemy made its save? Every area/save spell here already deals half on a save, never nothing.
  • Need a sure thing? Soul Arrow (Magic Missile) auto-hits.

So spend your casts on the moments that matter, not out of fear of whiffing.

A few extra conditions give Ashen its Dark Souls texture. Foundry tracks all of these automatically with token resources and effects.

Build-up statuses (Frostbite)

Some enemies — Vordt above all — do not hit you with a status outright; they build it up. Each hit adds a stack to a meter on your token. When the meter fills, the status triggers:

  • Frostbite — when the meter fills, you take a point on the Exhaustion track and the meter resets. Stacking Exhaustion is what turns Vordt's second phase into a cascade if the party clusters up.
  • Build-up fades over time if you avoid further hits, and clears completely at a bonfire.

Poise & Stagger

  • Poise comes from heavy armor and shields. While you carry meaningful Poise you ignore stagger and forced movement from minor hits, and a fully-armored defender may gain a second reaction each round (the DM's call).
  • Stagger is what a successful parry inflicts on an enemy: it loses its next action and opens a party-wide riposte. Blocking never staggers — not you, not the enemy — so a parry is the only thing that opens a riposte.

Embers (kindling)

An Ember is a consumable. Use one at a bonfire to become Embered: gain bonus maximum HP (a kindled glow) until you next die. On death — a full party wipe — the Ember's effect is lost, and you will want another to kindle again. Embers are the carrot for fighting well: stay un-wiped and you stay strong.

Unblockable moves

Some attacks — charges, shield-kicks, grabs, magic and true combos — are tagged unblockable: raising your shield does nothing against them. They are answered by movement — dodge, or sidestep the charge lane on its wind-up (W1). A move's card tells you: the Block row shows — when it cannot be blocked. Everything else can be blocked for your shield's set reduction, though a hit may still chip through, and a block never staggers anyone.

When something attacks you

  1. DM narrates the move in 3 windows: W1 wind-up · W2 commit · W3 impact.
  2. You declare one defense + one window: Dodge, Block, or Parry.
  3. Roll one flat d20 (no ability mod); your read sets advantage / normal / disadvantage.
DodgeFree, unlimited. Flat d20 vs DC. Success = no damage + move 5 ft (behind = advantage next attack).
BlockCosts reaction, no roll. Set % reduction per damage type (shield card); chips at low tiers, never staggers. Charges / grabs / shield-kicks = unblockable.
ParryCosts reaction. Flat d20 — one window is ADV, all others DIS. Success = negate + stagger + party riposte. Fail = full damage.

Resting

Only at a bonfire. Full HP, Estus, and all spell casts back; Exhaustion and build-up cleared; all non-boss enemies respawn.

Healing

Estus: action, 1 charge, heal 2d4+2. Start with 3 charges. Refills at bonfire only.

Death

Solo at 0 HP = normal death saves. Whole-party wipe = respawn at bonfire, enemies reset, carried souls drop as a bloodstain (reclaim it, or lose it on a second wipe).

Souls

One shared pool = money + XP. Spend at bonfires to bank them safely. Carried souls are at risk.

Leveling (L3 to 10)

Each level: +1 ability score OR 1 spell, plus automatic HP. Reaching 15 in a stat unlocks its milestone (STR Crushing Blow · DEX Extra Strike · INT/WIS/IW +1 spell cast · Vigor = pure HP). Weapon Art at L6 & L9. Tiers: T1 @ L4 · T2 @ L6 · T3 @ L9. Extra Attack (L5) automatic.

Spells

Each spell has its own casts — T1/T2/T3 = 2 / 1 / 0, +1 with your school's 15 milestone (so a Tier-3 spell needs the milestone to cast at all). Refill at bonfire. Need the right catalyst in hand (Staff / Talisman / Pyromancy Flame); damage & DC scale with INT / WIS / IW.
